title: Building Windows container images
body:
**Desired behavior**:
I would expect that when no `RUN` commands are present in a container file, `buildah build --os=windows` would be able to build a working Windows container image.

**Current state:**
In my testing I have found that buildah does not create functional Windows container images.
This seems to be due to at least two reasons:

1) The [required tar headers specified by the OCI image-spec](https://github.com/opencontainers/image-spec/blob/main/layer.md#platform-specific-attributes) are not being applied. 
([image-spec issue with slightly more info](https://github.com/opencontainers/image-spec/issues/415))
2) The rootfs of Windows container images is actually the `Files/` directory. This can be seen by pulling a Windows container image and inspecting the layers.

There may be other reasons, but if there are, they are not documented.

I have only tested this by building an image with a Linux build of buildah, with the image being used by containerd on a Windows VM.

I built two images, one using docker buildx, the other with buildah:

Using the following containerfile:
```
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/windows/nanoserver:ltsc2022
ADD emptyfile .
```
An image built using docker buildx works with no issue, containerd logs:
```
time="2022-05-20T16:46:52.533976500+02:00" level=info msg="PullImage \"quay.io/ssoto/test:nanoBuildx\""
time="2022-05-20T16:47:07.411314400+02:00" level=info msg="ImageCreate event &ImageCreate{Name:quay.io/ssoto/test:nanoBuildx,Labels:map[string]string{io.cri-containerd.image: managed,},XXX_unrecognized:[],}"
time="2022-05-20T16:47:07.422273200+02:00" level=info msg="ImageCreate event &ImageCreate{Name:sha256:43aba2088aa29238b8dc6ce66f9fdea52bc9401da781c03dc7c0bbfb5e9b0b67,Labels:map[string]string{io.cri-containerd.image: managed,},XXX_unrecognized:[],}"
time="2022-05-20T16:47:07.430670500+02:00" level=info msg="ImageUpdate event &ImageUpdate{Name:quay.io/ssoto/test:nanoBuildx,Labels:map[string]string{io.cri-containerd.image: managed,},XXX_unrecognized:[],}"
time="2022-05-20T16:47:07.438064300+02:00" level=info msg="ImageCreate event &ImageCreate{Name:quay.io/ssoto/test@sha256:fab9aeb28d8b11c65595143fcfb80a44a4f1a8d917b5559be981d8353011fde1,Labels:map[string]string{io.cri-containerd.image: managed,},XXX_unrecognized:[],}"
time="2022-05-20T16:47:07.438593200+02:00" level=info msg="PullImage \"quay.io/ssoto/test:nanoBuildx\" returns image reference \"sha256:43aba2088aa29238b8dc6ce66f9fdea52bc9401da781c03dc7c0bbfb5e9b0b67\""

```
 buildah built images cause the following in containerd logs when pulling the image:
```
time="2022-05-20T16:41:34.956100200+02:00" level=info msg="PullImage \"quay.io/ssoto/test:nanoShouldWork\""
time="2022-05-20T16:41:50.274201300+02:00" level=error msg="PullImage \"quay.io/ssoto/test:nanoShouldWork\" failed" error="failed to pull and unpack image \"quay.io/ssoto/test:nanoShouldWork\": failed to extract layer sha256:dff44eadebe5e0f7717289f95e61260f45df7951a5fc2adea46801dcded8ae6d: hcsshim::ImportLayer failed in Win32: The system cannot find the path specified. (0x3): unknown"
```

The difference between the two images can be seen in the image layer created from the `ADD` directive
buildkit added layer:
```
header: &{Typeflag:53 Name:Hives Linkname: Size:0 Mode:16384 Uid:0 Gid:0 Uname: Gname: ModTime:2022-05-20 10:45:06.607995006 -0400 EDT AccessTime:0001-01-01 00:00:00 +0000 UTC ChangeTime:0001-01-01 00:00:00 +0000 UTC Devmajor:0 Devminor:0 Xattrs:map[] PAXRecords:map[LIBARCHIVE.creationtime:1653057906.607995006 MSWIND
OWS.fileattr:16 mtime:1653057906.607995006] Format:PAX}
header: &{Typeflag:53 Name:Files Linkname: Size:0 Mode:16384 Uid:0 Gid:0 Uname: Gname: ModTime:2022-05-20 10:45:06.60847413 -0400 EDT AccessTime:0001-01-01 00:00:00 +0000 UTC ChangeTime:0001-01-01 00:00:00 +0000 UTC Devmajor:0 Devminor:0 Xattrs:map[] PAXRecords:map[LIBARCHIVE.creationtime:1653057906.608474130 MSWINDO
WS.fileattr:16 mtime:1653057906.60847413] Format:PAX}
header: &{Typeflag:48 Name:Files/emptyfile Linkname: Size:0 Mode:33204 Uid:0 Gid:0 Uname:root Gname:root ModTime:2022-05-20 09:46:28 -0400 EDT AccessTime:0001-01-01 00:00:00 +0000 UTC ChangeTime:0001-01-01 00:00:00 +0000 UTC Devmajor:0 Devminor:0 Xattrs:map[] PAXRecords:map[LIBARCHIVE.creationtime:1653054388.0 MSWIND
OWS.fileattr:32 MSWINDOWS.rawsd:AQAEgBQAAAAkAAAAAAAAADAAAAABAgAAAAAABSAAAAAgAgAAAQEAAAAAAAUSAAAAAgBMAAMAAAAAABgA/wEfAAECAAAAAAAFIAAAACACAAAAABQA/wEfAAEBAAAAAAAFEgAAAAAAGACpABIAAQIAAAAAAAUgAAAAIQIAAA==] Format:PAX}
```

buildah added layer:
```
header: &{Typeflag:48 Name:emptyfile Linkname: Size:0 Mode:33204 Uid:0 Gid:0 Uname:root Gname:root ModTime:2022-05-20 09:46:28 -0400 EDT AccessTime:0001-01-01 00:00:00 +0000 UTC ChangeTime:0001-01-01 00:00:00 +0000 UTC Devmajor:0 Devminor:0 Xattrs:map[] PAXRecords:map[] Format:USTAR}
```
**What I've tried:**
I've done some hacking on add.go, to try and see the minimal changes possible to make things work, mainly around to add Windows expected headers to a new directory I'm creating.
```
diff --git a/add.go b/add.go
index 8aa53a29..66032170 100644
--- a/add.go
+++ b/add.go
@@ -376,9 +376,66 @@ func (b *Builder) Add(destination string, extract bool, options AddAndCopyOption
        if err := copier.Mkdir(mountPoint, extractDirectory, mkdirOptions); err != nil {
                return errors.Wrapf(err, "error ensuring target directory exists")
        }
+       ////////
+       {
+               logrus.Infof("starting creating some directories")
+               var putErr error
+               var wg sync.WaitGroup
 
+               pipeReader, pipeWriter := io.Pipe()
+               wg.Add(1)
+               go func() {
+                       tw := tar.NewWriter(pipeWriter)
+                       defer tw.Close()
+                       hdr := tar.Header{
+                               Typeflag: tar.TypeDir,
+                               Name:     "testdir",
+                               Size:     0,
+                               Format:   tar.FormatPAX,
+                               Mode:     1 << 14,
+                               PAXRecords: map[string]string{
+                                       "MSWINDOWS.fileattr": "16",
+                                       "MSWINDOWS.rawsd":    "",
+                               },
+                       }
+                       err = tw.WriteHeader(&hdr)
+                       if err != nil {
+                               logrus.Infof("error %s", err)
+                       }
+                       pipeWriter.Close()
+                       wg.Done()
+               }()
+               wg.Add(1)
+               go func() {
+                       b.ContentDigester.Start("")
+                       hashCloser := b.ContentDigester.Hash()
+                       hasher := io.Writer(hashCloser)
+                       if options.Hasher != nil {
+                               hasher = io.MultiWriter(hasher, options.Hasher)
+                       }
+                       if options.DryRun {
+                               _, putErr = io.Copy(hasher, pipeReader)
+                       } else {
+                               putOptions := copier.PutOptions{
+                                       UIDMap:        destUIDMap,
+                                       GIDMap:        destGIDMap,
+                                       ChownDirs:     nil,
+                                       ChmodDirs:     nil,
+                                       ChownFiles:    nil,
+                                       ChmodFiles:    nil,
+                                       IgnoreDevices: userns.RunningInUserNS(),
+                               }
+                               putErr = copier.Put(extractDirectory, extractDirectory, putOptions, io.TeeReader(pipeReader, hasher))
+                       }
+                       hashCloser.Close()
+                       pipeReader.Close()
+                       wg.Done()
+               }()
+       }
+       ///////
```
Built and saved the resulting image
```
make buildah
./bin/buildah build --log-level=info -f Dockerfile.nano -t handmade:latest . 
./bin/buildah push handmade:latest dir:handmade
```

For a reason unknown to me, I can't get the header changes to stick. I have some ideas as to why this is happening but I'm not familiar enough with this codebase to fully figure this out. 

**Extra info:**
[What buildkit is doing differently from buildah](https://github.com/moby/buildkit/blob/3eed7fdf41c1fa626c35b3589b403c34dd3b2205/util/winlayers/differ.go#L206)
Related containerd issue: `https://github.com/containerd/containerd/issues/2469`

comments.body:
@nalind @flouthoc PTAL
I think one issue with this is the difference between a the file systems of Windows versus Linux, in that Windows requires file systems that are NOT case sensitive, while Linux file systems are. This can cause Linux to think files do not exist on Windows, which actually do in a different case.

I think we should add this part to buildah since headers are part of image-spec but I am a bit unsure what else is needed to make this work. @sebsoto Would adding headers to `ADD` would be enough for your use-case ? Could you also please let us know how to replay this part of your use-case so its easier to verify ?

Hi @flouthoc

My full use case is actually building Windows containers from scratch on a Linux build system, for deployment on OpenShift Windows nodes running containerd.

For the purpose of getting things off the ground its probably best to simplify it to:
Adding files to a Windows container image with buildah, from a working Windows base image, which can be pulled and ran by containerd on a Windows Server VM.

> Would adding headers to ADD would be enough for your use-case ?

I think adding the required PAX headers to files added with `ADD`, when building Windows images, would get things in a better place. It is the only requirement that is actually documented in the spec, and would get us to a point where undocumented issues with the rootfs could be worked around by changing the destination of the `ADD` commands.
 
For example a basic container file like this:
```
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/windows/nanoserver:ltsc2022
ADD hello-world.ps1 .
ENTRYPOINT ["powershell.exe",".\hello-world.ps1"]
```
Could be made into this
```
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/windows/nanoserver:ltsc2022
ADD hello-world.ps1 /Files/hello-world.ps1
ENTRYPOINT ["powershell.exe",".\hello-world.ps1"]
```
> Could you also please let us know how to replay this part of your use-case so its easier to verify ?

This can be done by building the above image, installing and running containerd on a Windows Server 2022 VM and pulling the resulting image with the ctr cmd line tool.
```
PS C:\Users\Administrator\bin> ./ctr.exe image pull quay.io/ssoto/test:buildahtest
quay.io/ssoto/test:buildahtest:                                                   resolved       |++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++|
manifest-sha256:1b41a2c2a3adeebd1e1cc3e2cebc0452fd73ab6891e1efd57ba14f64fc56c4fd: done           |++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++|
layer-sha256:d03441141c95eef78b79790dd4b9ccce555c2df4a334a1d6557819ee9217626f:    done           |++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++|
config-sha256:91ea0cb6af5c6d7b941f1aebc42d5c8442c961cc5cfa77191218c977eb27771d:   done           |++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++|
layer-sha256:ba9029e8f0db611e02610d88ba03fa2b22dff4c8bc0a03dbf0b44e4fb1703684:    done           |++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++|
elapsed: 3.6 s                                                                    total:  119.0  (33.0 MiB/s)
unpacking windows/amd64 sha256:1b41a2c2a3adeebd1e1cc3e2cebc0452fd73ab6891e1efd57ba14f64fc56c4fd...
time="2022-05-24T15:31:13+02:00" level=info msg="apply failure, attempting cleanup" error="failed to extract layer sha256:f24ea451fdc7d67143e0d3509b1cf77a0783e47b8a4e21fa4731c717c1b9a757: hcsshim::ImportLayer failed in Win32: The system cannot find the path specified. (0x3): unknown" key="extract-893564400-DvyI sha256:d09d2d03f098739eefa63f0734314203baeb53640b3a8067bed4439a0c1805c6"
ctr: failed to extract layer sha256:f24ea451fdc7d67143e0d3509b1cf77a0783e47b8a4e21fa4731c717c1b9a757: hcsshim::ImportLayer failed in Win32: The system cannot find the path specified. (0x3): unknown
```
You can see that Windows is unable to extract and import the layer. 

For preliminary local testing I have been using a small go program to inspect headers present in the image layers:
```
package main

import (
        "archive/tar"
        "fmt"
        "io"
        "os"
)

func main() {
        fmt.Println("hello world")
        if len(os.Args) != 2 {
                fmt.Println("missing expected tar file path")
                os.Exit(1)
        }
        tarFile := os.Args[1]
        fp, err := os.Open(tarFile)
        if err != nil {
                fmt.Println(err)
                os.Exit(1)
        }
        tarReader := tar.NewReader(fp)
        for {
                header, err := tarReader.Next()
                if err != nil {
                        if err == io.EOF {
                                os.Exit(0)
                        }
                        fmt.Printf("hit error %s\n", err)
                        os.Exit(1)
                }
                fmt.Printf("header: %+v\n", header)
        }
}
```

Setting additional headers and munging paths when we generate layer diffs is certainly doable, but when I try to build a sample with `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build --progress=plain --platform windows/amd64`, I'm currently getting this error at the very end:
```
#9 exporting to image
#9 sha256:e8c613e07b0b7ff33893b694f7759a10d42e180f2b4dc349fb57dc6b71dcab00
#9 exporting layers 0.0s done
#9 writing image sha256:cea4dfc79aaf0bf2a705666b97274323e27db955c833b692a9df6bf8d2e79090 done
#9 ERROR: operating system is not supported
------
 > exporting to image:
------
operating system is not supported
```
That means we can't compare what we generate for various instructions and what `docker build` does as part of a conformance test, which would try to do the equivalent via the engine API.

@nalind 
building Windows images on linux can be done through `docker buildx`
```
$ sudo /usr/bin/docker buildx create --name img-builder --use --platform windows/amd64
$ sudo /usr/bin/docker buildx build --platform windows/amd64 --output=type=registry -f buildahtest  -t quay.io/ssoto/test:dockerxtest .
[+] Building 2.3s (9/9) FINISHED                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
 => [internal] booting buildkit                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          0.4s
 => => starting container buildx_buildkit_img-builder                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    0.4s
 => [internal] load build definition from buildahtest                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    0.0s
 => => transferring dockerfile: 220B                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     0.0s
 => [internal] load .dockerignore                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        0.0s

 => => transferring context: 119B                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        0.0s
 => CACHED [1/2] FROM mcr.microsoft.com/windows/nanoserver:ltsc2022@sha256:f677b64d44434c6850f075630c3a7b3f9be284d32003c2cc6ea93130f619152f                                                                                                                                                                              0.0s
 => => resolve mcr.microsoft.com/windows/nanoserver:ltsc2022@sha256:f677b64d44434c6850f075630c3a7b3f9be284d32003c2cc6ea93130f619152f                                                                                                                                                                                     0.0s
 => [2/2] ADD hello-world.ps1 .                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          0.1s
 => exporting to image                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   1.5s
 => => exporting layers                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  0.1s
 => => exporting manifest sha256:6f1e952761892222bf6e838720b1156cdda72939002f4bfbd599459f73ef0fee                                                                                                                                                                                                                        0.0s
 => => exporting config sha256:98c382ab4b69139b456fb1064842e40ddb45f9167a5a76bd414147e6b986d341                                                                                                                                                                                                                          0.0s
 => => pushing layers                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    1.2s
 => => pushing manifest for quay.io/ssoto/test:dockerxtest@sha256:6f1e952761892222bf6e838720b1156cdda72939002f4bfbd599459f73ef0fee                                                                                                                                                                                       0.2s
 => [auth] ssoto/test:pull,push token for quay.io      
```
Oh I believe you, but without being able to compare our output during automated tests, it'll be very difficult to verify that we did it right, or be able to notice if it breaks in the future.

Just to experiment I was trying to modify these headers in `copier` but something similar is happening with me i am not able to get it to stick. Maybe its something related to header size. Need to try a few more things.

@sebsoto I was able to get it working please try this patch here: https://github.com/containers/buildah/pull/4053 and leave a review if you think something needs to be changed. Unfortunately this can't be merged since buildah CI is not capable to test windows patches as of now.
@flouthoc thanks I will try it out and let you know

Reply to this and moving discussion here: https://github.com/containers/buildah/pull/4053#issuecomment-1158025730

> I've tried this out, and on a Linux build system, I was able to build a Windows container image and run it on a Windows server using containerd.
> 
> I understand there are CI limitations, but it would be great if this work could make it in, with the proper detection for Windows/Linux container images.

Hi @sebsoto Thanks for trying and verifying this patch :smile: . I'll try to prepare a test for this but from discussion with maintainers we were unable to decide priority on this one. 

Could you let us know who is using this feature and what would be the priority. 

Thanks

@flouthoc 
This feature would be used by the OpenShift Windows containers team. It is one of a few blockers stopping us from building Windows containers within OpenShift CI, as well as in our release pipeline. To me, this priority is medium-high, as it is not currently the only blocker, but getting past this issue would be very helpful for us to determine potential timelines of product features we are trying to release.
Do you have any way we could test these.  We have no problem accepting this functionality, but without a test, we would have no idea if another PR breaks windows support.

Adding a unit test for this seems easy we can check PAX headers on each layer, i think we can also test on windows if any less maintenance github action is available. I'll try checking this and if we get tests for this this can be merged upstream.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@flouthoc DId you ever work on this?

Hi @flouthoc @rhatdan
Any progress on this? Having this functionality in buildah would be of great use.
@sebsoto I will look at it this week, was not able to look at it in the last week.
Thanks!

@rhatdan @flouthoc @umohnani8 any progress on this? We would like to start using [host process containers](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/create-hostprocess-pod/), and this is the first step that will allow us to do so as we need to be able to use buildah to build Windows images. Alternative would be to start investigating dockerx.

@aravindhp is building on Linux and running on Windows still a part of the plan?  A big chunk of this becomes easier to follow if we're not trying to do cross-OS builds - platform-specific APIs would let us at least be able to identify what we're putting into headers, which would be vastly preferable over stuffing in a (for all purposes) opaque binary value and hoping for the best.

> is building on Linux and running on Windows still a part of the plan? 

@nalind I am afraid so 😞 The issue is that if we need a Windows instance to build this, then adding support for bringing up Windows instances in Brew and CI is going to be tough (impossible due to licensing issues???). @sebsoto please correct if I am wrong.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

@nalind @rhatdan 

We've reached a point where running Windows build systems in our CI may be doable.
Would adding support for building Windows containers on Windows build systems be possible?
I'm happy to help contribute to make this happen.

> I think it would likely best to start with some high level overview of what you are going to change and how buildah on windows should work. We have very little windows expertise so any actual review will be very difficult us. Thus I am not sure how that would work out.
I am not strictly against it but I think this needs some actual plan first. How is going to do all that work and then also who is maintaining it going forward? (It would be best to keep that discussion on https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues/4010).

@Luap99 
Answering this here.
I think the starting point here is to get unit tests on Windows running in CI, and incrementally fix them. Making them required as they get to passing so no regressions occur.

I have some local changes that was able to complete a build on Windows with a simple FROM + COPY containerfile. Its mainly minor stuff having to do with filepaths not being handled properly. I can open PRs to get that functionality merged upstream. I wasn't able to test if the container is runnable, as `buildah push` wasn't functional, and I haven't wanted to commit too much work to this if it is for sure not going to be accepted.

Did this conversation progress in any direction?

There is this issue, suggesting making Windows better supported — and things like https://github.com/containers/storage/pull/2284 , suggesting dropping the existing stubs. Which one do we want?

I opened up https://github.com/containers/buildah/pull/6334 as an initial proposal for what can be done to address this issue. 

The scope of this is limited to COPY commands. 

Before working on adding testing, I would appreciate some feedback for if this is the appropriate way to implement this.


I manually tested this with the following Containerfile which builds the azurefile CSI plugin from this commit https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/azurefile-csi-driver/tree/2036d65797d6aca2a15d2024042688ef8415f166

```
FROM brew.registry.redhat.io/rh-osbs/openshift-golang-builder:rhel_9_1.24 as build
LABEL stage=build
COPY . /src
WORKDIR /src
RUN ls
RUN make azurefile-windows#https://github.com/microsoft/windows-host-process-containers-base-image
FROM --platform=windows/amd64 mcr.microsoft.com/oss/kubernetes/windows-host-process-containers-base-image:v1.0.0
LABEL description="CSI Azure file plugin"ARG ARCH=amd64
ARG binary=/src/_output/${ARCH}/azurefileplugin.exe
COPY --from=build ${binary} /azurefileplugin.exe
ENV PATH="C:\Windows\system32;C:\Windows;C:\WINDOWS\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\;"
USER ContainerAdministrator
ENTRYPOINT ["/azurefileplugin.exe"]

``` 

The command used was: `../buildah/bin/buildah  bud -f pkg/azurefileplugin/WindowsHostProcess.Dockerfile -t quay.io/ssoto/test:azurefile-csi`.

This works 50% of the runs. Every other run the following error is seen. I am not able to debug it without help from the containers team.

`Error: committing container for step {Env:[ARCH=amd64 binary=/src/_output/amd64/azurefileplugin.exe PATH=C:\Windows\system32;C:\Windows;C:\WINDOWS\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\;] Command:entrypoint Args:[/azurefileplugin.exe] Flags:[] Attrs:map[json:true] Message:ENTRYPOINT /azurefileplugin.exe Heredocs:[] Original:ENTRYPOINT ["/azurefileplugin.exe"]}: copying layers and metadata for container "e1fd0e9067beff55d3c035bad92fa95f8f85c2e24bd1134c0a2f9c58a4f597de": writing blob: storing blob to file "/var/tmp/container_images_storage267240268/1": happened during read: Digest did not match, expected sha256:5af8b18a5000a8ae0acd92b07423aa2d76c6ffc942d67bafbd707e6c5c9234bd, got sha256:75e89213738f5edef02efa7476096fc1cddb206da8e1e166ec747d304fb56ee9`

I was able to successfully use the built image as a Windows container.

For adding tests for this approach, I think adding a test to validate the appropriate PAX headers are there for a Windows build would be fine. At the moment I don't think the complexity of actually testing the image on a Windows VM is needed.
Thank you @sebsoto a ton!
